title: Complete Joy
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro

outline.title: Introduction: Context of the Letter and the Sermon
subpoints: Second John is a brief note written to hold things over until John can come in person — likely to the church at Ephesus
The 'elect lady' is a veiled reference to a local church struggling with false teachers
John desires to address the church's unity issues face to face rather than in writing
This Sunday the church baptizes the Thomas children — a shorter sermon to allow time for that occasion

outline.title: Differences in Relationships Are Better Solved in Person
subpoints: John sees hints of disunity in the church — 'some' are walking in truth (v.4), implying others are not
He wants to speak face to face because conflicts are resolved more easily in person
Jesus commands reconciliation before worship: go to your brother first (Matthew 5:23-24)
As followers of Jesus Christ, we are not allowed to carry unreconciled relationships — we must strive to reconcile

outline.title: Face-to-Face Reconciliation Produces Mutual Joy
subpoints: John says speaking face to face will make 'our joy full' — reconciliation results in joy, not just resolution
Luke 15 presents three parables all built around restoration and the joy it produces
The lost sheep: joy in heaven over one sinner who repents (Luke 15:7)
The lost son: the father runs to him; joy breaks out in celebration — even so loud the older son hears it from the field

outline.title: Practical Urgency: Broken Relationships Are a Spiritual Problem
subpoints: Unresolved relationships create bitterness — and nothing is more contrary to joy than bitterness (Hebrews 12:14-15)
As much as it depends on us, we are to live peaceably with all people (Romans 12:18)
Refusal to be reconciled is sin against God and a black eye on the gospel
Our Savior prayed for our unity the night before his betrayal — the Father always listens to the Son (John 17:21)

outline.title: The Way We Relate to One Another Is a Testimony to Other Churches
subpoints: Verse 13: the children of the elect sister (another church) greet them — other churches observe us
We are part of something far bigger than our four walls; we belong to the church throughout the world and throughout the ages
Christ's prayer for unity (John 17:21) is a world-saving testimony: that the world may believe
One of the ways we bless other churches is by working on our own relationships within this church

summary:
In this sermon on the conclusion of 2 John (verses 12-13), Pastor Tito Lyro introduces the passage within its context: John is writing a brief letter to hold things over until he can visit the struggling church in person. The occasion for the sermon is also the baptism of the Thomas children, giving the message a celebratory and relational texture.

The sermon develops two main lessons. First, differences in relationships are better resolved face to face. John wants to come to the church in person because written communication is inadequate for relational reconciliation. Jesus himself commands face to face engagement in Matthew 5 (go to the altar and leave your gift — go to your brother first) and Matthew 18 (if your brother sins against you, go to him). The Holy Spirit, when inspiring these commands, was fully aware of Zoom, FaceTime, and texting — and still said 'go and talk with them.' Followers of Jesus are not permitted to carry unreconciled relationships as far as it depends on them.

Second, face-to-face reconciliation produces mutual joy. John says speaking in person will make 'our joy full.' The sermon draws on Luke 15's three parables — the lost sheep, the lost coin, and the prodigal son — to demonstrate the explosive joy that accompanies restoration. Just as heaven rejoices over one sinner who repents, the church erupts with joy when broken human relationships are restored through the gospel. Unresolved relationships, by contrast, produce bitterness — the root that defiles many (Hebrews 12:14-15).

The sermon closes by noting that verse 13 — the greetings from another church — reminds us that our relationships are watched. We belong to the universal and historic church; our unity (or lack of it) is a testimony. Christ prayed for a unity so profound that the world would believe — and the Father always answers the Son. The call is to pursue reconciled, joy-filled relationships as a witness to the gospel.
transcript:
## Context: A Brief Note and an Upcoming Visit

We read today the conclusion of the letter of Second John — verses 12 and 13. John wrote this brief letter to hold things together in the church until he could come in person and speak face to face. Scholars believe the "elect lady" addressed in the letter refers to a local church, likely connected to Ephesus, which was struggling with false teachers who did not acknowledge who Jesus truly was. John had written plainly: if a teacher does not confess what the Bible teaches concerning Christ, he does not know God — put him out, do not support his ministry, have nothing to do with him.

As we consider verses 12 and 13, two truths emerge: **differences in a relationship are better resolved in person**, and **the way we relate to one another is a testimony to other churches**.

## Differences Are Better Resolved in Person

> *"Having many things to write to you, I do not wish to do so with paper and ink; but I hope to come to you and speak face to face, that our joy may be full."* (2 John 12)

There are hints of disunity in this letter. In verse 4, John says he rejoiced that *some* of the church's members were walking in truth — which implies that some were not. In verse 5 he repeats the command to love one another, suggesting that love was being neglected. Conflict was present in the church, and John wanted to address it directly, in person, because conflicts are more easily resolved face to face.

Jesus himself is emphatic about this. In Matthew 5:23–24 he says:

> *"If you bring your gift to the altar, and there remember that your brother has something against you, leave your gift there before the altar, and go your way. First be reconciled to your brother, and then come and offer your gift."*

The urgency is remarkable: the need for reconciliation takes priority even over the act of worship. The point carries to Matthew 18:15, where Jesus says:

> *"If your brother sins against you, go and tell him his fault between you and him alone. If he hears you, you have gained your brother."*

Matthew 5: you know that your brother has something against you — go to him. Matthew 18: your brother has sinned against you — go to him. In both cases the command is the same: go, approach, speak face to face, and seek reconciliation.

It is worth noting that when the Holy Spirit inspired these words, he was fully aware of every technology that would be available to us today — video calls, text messages, email. And yet the command is not "communicate with them in some way." It is "go and talk with them." That is what the Holy Spirit intends.

As followers of Jesus Christ, we are not permitted — as far as it depends on us — to carry unreconciled relationships. We are to strive to reconcile every relationship in our lives.

And when that reconciliation happens — when we sit face to face and make peace by the power of the Spirit — the result is mutual joy. John says: *"that our joy may be full."*

## The Joy of Restored Relationships

Restored relationships produce great joy because they mirror the greatest restoration of all: our reconciliation to God.

In Luke 15, Jesus gives three parables of things lost and found. In the parable of the lost sheep, the shepherd recovers one sheep from a hundred:

> *"There will be more joy in heaven over one sinner who repents than over ninety-nine just persons who need no repentance."* (Luke 15:7)

In the parable of the lost coin, a woman searches her whole house to find one missing coin, and when she finds it, her neighbors rejoice with her:

> *"There is joy in the presence of the angels of God over one sinner who repents."* (Luke 15:10)

And in the parable of the prodigal son, the father sees his returning son from a distance, runs to meet him, and throws a feast:

> *"For this my son was dead and is alive again; he was lost and is found."* (Luke 15:24)

The party was so loud that the elder son heard it from the field. That is the joy of restoration.

David understood this: *"Behold, how good and how pleasant it is for brethren to dwell together in unity!"* (Psalm 133:1) And Jesus himself prayed for it:

> *"That they all may be one, as You, Father, are in Me, and I in You; that they also may be one in Us, that the world may believe that You sent Me."* (John 17:21)

This does not mean Jesus is praying for an end to denominations — Paul acknowledges in 1 Corinthians 11 that divisions exist and can be appropriate. But Jesus is praying for a unity in the gospel and a relational unity among believers so that the world sees him in our relationships.

Brothers and sisters, a refusal to be reconciled — a refusal to do whatever you ethically can to be at peace with people in your life — is a black eye on the gospel of Jesus Christ. Paul says:

> *"If it is possible, as much as depends on you, live peaceably with all men."* (Romans 12:18)

And the writer of Hebrews warns:

> *"Pursue peace with all people, and holiness, without which no one will see the Lord: looking carefully lest anyone fall short of the grace of God; lest any root of bitterness springing up cause trouble, and by this many become defiled."* (Hebrews 12:14–15)

Unresolved relationships create bitterness. Nothing is more contrary to joy than bitterness. Are there broken relationships in your life that remain so because you have not tried to restore them? If so, you are sinning against God. As much as it depends on you, you are to live at peace with all people.

## Our Unity Testifies to Other Churches

> *"The children of your elect sister greet you. Amen."* (2 John 13)

The "elect sister" is another church, from which John was writing, and her members were watching what was happening in the struggling church. Other churches notice how we relate to one another. They are interested and they are observing.

We are part of something much larger than the four walls of our building. We are part of the Church of the living God, across the world and across the ages. On the Lord's Day, worship is happening somewhere on earth every hour of every day, like a wave moving around the globe. We are part of that.

And one of the ways we bless other churches is by working faithfully on our own relationships here. How is it with you? How many broken relationships do you have in your life, and how many of those remain broken because you have not yet tried to restore them?

The beauty of the gospel is that it takes only one thing: repentance. We turn to Christ with the strength to obey him and set out, as much as is in us, to live at peace with all people.

Differences in relationships are better resolved in person. The way we relate to one another is a testimony to other churches. And all of this the night before he was betrayed, our Savior prayed that we would be united as one, as he is united to the Father. The Father always listens to the Son. He will bring us together — if not fully in this life, then in the life to come, when together with the whole church throughout the ages we will worship the Lamb who was slain and now lives and reigns.
